text: The surgeon insufflates carbon dioxide into the preperitoneal plane, revealing the white posterior rectus sheath. The cow web spaces open up as the avascular plane is developed, with careful attention to stay above the shiny posterior sheath.

The surgeon performs telescopic dissection to develop the pre-peritoneal space, carefully identifying the posterior rectus sheath and a small branch of the inferior epigastric vessel. The vessels are retained towards the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring the inferior epigastric vessel remains on the roof to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon identifies anatomical planes under vision, continuing dissection in the avascular space while ensuring the rectus muscle is covered by a small fascia. The posterior rectus sheath is visible, and the fascia becomes thin below the umbilicus, requiring careful dissection to avoid puncturing the peritoneum. The surgeon navigates between the layers of the transversalis fascia, observing the rectus muscle and fascia during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a controlled forward and backward movement with the telescope to minimize the risk of iatrogenic injury to the peritoneum. The dissection reaches the pubic bone at the midline, and the surgeon proceeds medially towards the opposite side to create a wider dissection space. Gentle movements are employed during lateral dissection to avoid peritoneal defects and maintain pneumoperitoneum.

The key to perform a good TEP is a very good understanding of the anatomy and attention to small details. Because these small finer details will enable us to perform the surgery without much of a trouble. the other important thing in the tep repair is to first develop your spaces the medial space or the retropubic space of ridges and the lateral space of bull grows and only after developing these spaces should we embark on the section of the sac because once we develop these two spaces even if there is a small oxygenic injury to the peritoneum defect which is leading to loss of peritoneum it doesn't really sorry loss of pneumo it doesn't really affect our progress in the surgery because the spaces are well developed we can still manage to complete the surgery even without any need for addition of an additional port or a various needle into the abdomen to remove the pneumo peritoneum which is going and created

The surgeon identifies a direct hernia and enters an avascular plane lateral to the pubic ligament. The inferior epigastric vessels come into view as the surgeon carefully dissects close to them, using force to separate the fascia and expand the lateral space. The edge of the peritoneum is visible just below the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate ligament and observes the fossa going anteriorly and fusing. The vessel is pushed laterally to facilitate the insertion of a working port, aiming for optimal triangulation. A small needle is introduced to ensure the peritoneum is not breached, followed by the insertion of the port under direct vision.

The surgeon uses a low-profile hook instrument with low monopolar wattage to minimize fumes while releasing thick fibrous strands and adhesions with cautery. The dissection proceeds in the avascular plane, aided by carbon dioxide insufflation at a pressure of twelve millimeters. A vessel from the corona mortis, an anastomotic vein on the cupous ligament between the external iliac vein and the obturator vein, is visible. The surgeon employs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to identify the correct planes and prevent bleeding.

The surgeon carefully crosses the midline using short bursts of cautery and blunt dissection, working to create space on the opposite side for triangulation. The thin fascia of the patient is visible, and the rectus muscle appears anteriorly. The surgeon prepares to introduce a working port under vision for mesh fixation using laparoscopic suturing.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to reveal avascular planes, applying traction to avoid bleeding while dissecting towards the opposite side. The inferior epigastric vessels are kept in the roof to prevent them from being dissected to the floor. Short bursts of cautery are used to release fibrotic strands during the dissection.

The surgeon uses a short burst of cautery and blunt dissection to open up the planes, pushing the peritoneum down and expanding the lateral space. The dissection progresses to the lateral border of the left rectus muscle, with careful attention to preserving a small fascia on the muscle to ensure the inferior epigastric vessels remain on the roof and are not displaced downward.

The surgeon performs further dissection, encountering fibrotic tissues between two fascia layers, likely due to previous episodes of irreducibility or strangulation. Using sharp dissection with low monopolar cautery, the fibrotic attachments are gradually removed, revealing a clear potential space. The dissection proceeds adjacent and below the pubic symphysis, aiming to separate the bladder from the anterior abdominal wall and ensure proper mesh placement without rolling. The fibrotic strands and fat are cleared to visualize the pubic symphysis and the arch of the bone clearly.

The pubic symphysis is visible as the surgeon continues dissection to expose the entire cupus ligament, which appears as a white shiny structure reflecting light, known as the lighthouse sign. The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to open up the planes and remove fibrous attachments, ensuring the ligament is fully exposed for mesh fixation later in the procedure.

The surgeon meticulously dissects fibrotic attachments to open up the lateral space, staying close to the inferior epigastric vessels. Sharp and blunt dissection techniques are used to identify white planes in the confined space, while maintaining low monopolar energy to minimize fogging. The lateral space is rapidly enlarged.

The surgeon releases the midline attachment, making the dissection easier. A small vessel is identified and dissected carefully to avoid trauma to the peritoneum. Fibrotic strands are taken down using a sharp C-monopolar hook, with coagulation applied to separate the fused peritoneum. The edge of the peritoneum is not clearly visible, requiring careful dissection under vision with short bursts of energy.

The surgeon opens up the lateral space and inserts working instruments at or below the umbilicus, staying medial to the inferior epigastric vessels to avoid trauma. Dissection continues towards the camera port to ensure proper placement of the mesh against the muscle without crumpling. Loose areolar tissue is dissected, revealing the arcuate ligament on the left side. A needle is introduced to identify direction and orientation.

A 5mm working port is inserted on the left side, with its tip clearly visualized. The surgeon uses the assisting port to further dissect the lateral space of Bogros. The instrument provides traction and counter-traction, allowing the planes to open up smoothly. An atraumatic instrument in the left hand acts like a surgical finger, applying pressure to identify planes and grasp tissues when necessary. The edge of the peritoneum becomes visible, and the fascia covering the nerves on the lateral side is preserved. The surgeon identifies the edge of the peritoneum and dissects between two avascular planes, using traction and counter-traction to release attachments without causing bleeding.

The surgeon releases fibrotic attachments near the arc width line to ensure the lateral edge of the mesh seats comfortably without rolling. The dissection is performed close to the inferior epigastric vessels, avoiding trauma and bleeding. The pubic bone and arch are visible, along with the bladder. The direct area is free of hernia, and a large indirect hernia is identified. The peritoneal edge and hernia sac are visible, with the surgeon dissecting and pushing the thin fibrovascular fascia around the sac, enhancing its visibility.

The surgeon divides the transversalis fascia fibers to expose the triangle of doom, allowing for the dissection of the vas deferens and testicular vessels. The pseudosac is visible and is being pushed upwards. The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, employing an atraumatic medline grasper to progress the dissection while occasionally using cautery.

The surgeon begins the sac dissection, moving from lateral to medial, revealing the white shiny vas deferens within the dissection field. The sac is nearly completely dissected, allowing for potential hooking or division at the internal ring. The sac is skeletonized, and the surgeon carefully progresses medially, avoiding electrical energy near the vas deferens, using blunt dissection and short bursts of energy as needed.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to carefully isolate the testicular vessels and vas from the hernia sac. The bluish peritoneum is visible, indicating complete dissection around the sac. The fascia is opened to ensure the sac is free and can be manipulated without concern. The wide sac is visible, and fibrous tissue is dissected to avoid trauma to the vas.

The surgeon isolates a large indirect hernia sac using short bursts of monopolar energy and blunt dissection, ensuring that the testicular vessels and vas deferens are pushed away from the sac. The thin fascia between the sac and the testicular structures is expanded, allowing for rapid dissection. The surgeon gains control over the vas and testicular vessels, retracts the sac, and uses energy to push away sling fibers, facilitating identification and retraction of the sac's fundus.

The surgeon skeletonizes the hernia sac, ensuring no bleeding occurs during dissection. The vas deferens and testicular vessels are carefully kept away from the dissection area to prevent injury. Transversalis sling fibers are dissected to improve sac exposure and anatomical identification. Monopolar energy is used to control a small vein causing minor bleeding. The surgeon continues to adjust the position on the peritoneum to maintain clear visibility of the sac and surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses sharp bursts of cautery to further dissect the hernia sac, ensuring no injury to the vas deferens or testicular vessels. The fundus of the sac is identified as a bulgy U-shaped structure, with small fibrous attachments being removed for a comprehensive dissection. The dissection progresses towards the end of the fundus, nearly completing the isolation of the sac.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the large hernia sac extending into the scrotum, carefully releasing the final fibrous attachments at the fundus. The sac is held in the surgeon's hands, and the camera view is adjusted to clearly visualize the fibrous planes. Short bursts of energy are used to coagulate and minimize bleeding, ensuring the vas and cord structures are safely pushed away from the dissection area.

The surgeon releases the last few fibrous strands between the fundus and card sections, freeing the entire sac. The sac descends into the abdominal area due to carbon dioxide insufflation, with no injury to the peritoneum observed.

The surgeon carefully dissects the edge of the peritoneum, ensuring it is pushed away to allow comfortable seating of the mesh. The lateral space is widened, with caution taken to avoid injury to the peritoneum. Smoke circulates within the surgical field, causing fogging. The transversalis fascia attachment forming the arcuate ligament is divided, and traction is applied to the pseudo sac. Short bursts of energy are used to manage vascular areas, revealing the periphery of the defect. The surgeon dissects between the inferior epigastric vessel, pushing the peritoneum down to create lateral space.

The surgeon dissects loose areolar tissue near the inferior epigastric vessel, ensuring to stay in the correct plane to avoid injury to the peritoneum. The edge of the mesh and the vas deferens are visible, with traction applied medially to safely divide thin fascial tissues near the external iliac vein.

The surgeon carefully applies medial traction to separate tissues from the external iliac vein, which appears as a blue structure to the right of the instrument. The external iliac artery, a white tubular structure, is visible laterally. The surgeon continues to dissect and open the lateral space of Bogros, while preserving the geolux fascia to prevent nerve exposure.

The surgeon continues dissection in an avascular plane, using blunt dissection to separate thin fascia from the clearly visible white peritoneal edge. A bulge indicates a small dent on the peritoneum, but dissection progresses without concern. Short bursts of energy are applied to dissect the peritoneum completely, while the vas is pushed away to prevent interference. The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt techniques to minimize oozing and maintain a clean surgical field.

The surgeon continues a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to mobilize the peritoneum, staying close to the peritoneal edge in an avascular field. The external iliac vein is visible, showing pulsation and collapse due to carbon dioxide insufflation. A small defect in the peritoneum is grasped below the instrument, with further dissection carried out laterally.

In the left hypochondrial region at Palmer's point, the surgeon uses an instrument to grasp and meticulously dissect the peritoneum, revealing its entire edge. The dissection is carried out carefully to avoid complications, and an endo loop is prepared for application to a moderate-sized defect.

The surgeon applies an endo loop to secure the peritoneal defect, ensuring the mesh remains extraperitonealized to prevent direct contact with the viscera. The peritoneum is well mobilized on the lateral side, and further dissection is performed to release it from the anterior abdominal wall, facilitating proper mesh seating and preventing shrinkage or recurrence.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to release the peritoneum from the anterior abdominal wall, widening the lateral space for mesh placement. The transversalis fascia is released, and the lateral space is visibly wide and well-dissected. The surgeon coagulates minor bleeding and continues pushing the peritoneum away until the vas deferens is seen turning medially.

The surgeon completes the dissection as the vas deferens turns medially, marking the limit for peritoneal mobilization. The external iliac vein is visible, and the area is prepared for placing a 15x12 cm lightweight polypropylene mesh.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the fascia layer to expose the obturator vessels and nerve, identifying a peritoneal defect. An instrument is used to grasp the defect, and an endo loop is applied around it. The loop is tightened, obscuring the rent, while ensuring no additional defects are present.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect tissues and the hernia sac, creating a wide space for mesh placement. The peritoneum is pushed low to ensure adequate space. The vas deferens is identified as it turns medially, indicating successful dissection and mobilization of the peritoneum. The external iliac artery is visible with pulsations, and dissection continues on the left side to mirror the right side, ensuring the mesh will sit comfortably.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to meticulously dissect adjacent to the edge of the peritoneum, clearly visible and pushed away, ensuring no trauma or defects occur. The dissection progresses from the lateral space towards the anterior abdominal wall, creating a space for the mesh to sit comfortably, crucial for preventing recurrence.

The surgeon identifies the dissected area on the left side, revealing an indirect hernia without a sac, minimizing the risk of seroma formation. The testicular vessels and vas deferens are visible medially, with the triangle of doom situated between them, containing the external iliac artery. The inferior epigastric vessel is seen medial to the widened internal ring, confirming the presence of an indirect inguinal hernia. The pubic bone, pubic arch, and symphysis are clearly shown. Additionally, a small direct hernia is pointed out, with the inferior epigastric vessel lateral to it, indicating a direct inguinal hernia through the Hesselbach's triangle.

The surgeon identifies the hernial defect and points out the external iliac vein and artery using an instrument. The vas is observed turning medially and entering the indirect sac, alongside the testicular vessels forming the triangle of doom. A lightweight polypropylene mesh is rolled and sutured for easier handling, then rapidly placed into position. The mesh, measuring fifteen by thirteen centimeters, is positioned with curved cuts for the psoas muscle and prostate, ensuring a minimum overlap of two centimeters beyond the pubic symphysis.

The surgeon uses a proline suture to take a superficial bite on the white, shiny Cooper's ligament, ensuring the mesh remains firm against the abdominal wall. The needle stays firm after placement, indicating a good bite. The surgeon knots the suture loosely to secure the mesh without causing excessive tension, which could lead to chronic pain.

The surgeon unrolls the remaining two-thirds of the mesh against the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring it is seated comfortably in the dissected space. The mesh is fixed with a Prolene suture through the Cooper's ligament, which is visibly stretched as the bite is taken. The mesh is deployed similarly on both sides, with overlapping edges visible below the pubic area.
